definition: From H6342; a (sudden) alarm (properly, the object feared, by implication, the feeling): — dread(-ful), fear, (thing) great (fear, -ly feared), terror.

semantic_categories.name: 1. Sudden Dread / Visceral Terror
semantic_categories.description: The core sense of pachad: a sudden, startling alarm that seizes a person physically. Unlike yare (H3372), which spans the full spectrum from terror to reverence, pachad emphasizes the involuntary, visceral nature of the fear response — trembling, physical shaking, the feeling of being ambushed by dread.

semantic_categories.name: 2. The Object of Dread / The Thing Feared
semantic_categories.description: Pachad frequently denotes not just the emotion but the object that causes it — the feared thing itself. The definition notes this explicitly: 'properly, the object feared.' This objective sense appears when pachad stands as the subject that falls upon people, rather than the feeling they experience.

semantic_categories.name: 3. Pachad as Divine Title ("The Fear of Isaac")
semantic_categories.description: A unique theological usage where pachad functions as a divine name — 'the Fear of Isaac' (pachad yitschaq). This identifies God as the One whom Isaac reverenced, using the word for dread itself as a title. It appears only in Genesis 31 in the Jacob-Laban narrative, where Jacob swears by the God his father feared.

semantic_categories.name: 4. Dread as an Instrument of Divine Power
semantic_categories.description: Pachad describes the terror God sends upon nations and enemies as a weapon of deliverance. The dread falls upon people groups, paralyzing them before Israel. This overlaps with eimah (H367) but pachad emphasizes the sudden, overwhelming quality of the terror.

semantic_categories.name: 5. Nighttime / Ambush Dread
semantic_categories.description: Several pachad passages specifically connect it to fear that strikes at night or by surprise — the terror that comes without warning. This aligns with the root meaning of sudden alarm.

semantic_categories.name: 6. Freedom from Pachad (Promised Security)
semantic_categories.description: Wisdom literature and prophetic texts promise deliverance from pachad as a sign of divine protection and trust. The absence of sudden dread marks the state of the righteous who trust God.

collocations.pattern: pachad YHWH — the fear/dread of the LORD falling on people
pachad + night — dread striking in darkness
pachad falling upon — dread descending on enemies
pachad coming upon — dread arriving suddenly
pachad Yitschaq — the Fear of Isaac (divine title)

synonyms.distinction: eimah = crushing, paralyzing terror; pachad = sudden alarm/startled dread
yare = broad fear spectrum (terror through reverence); pachad = the visceral, startling end of that spectrum
charad = trembling/shuddering fear; similar physical emphasis but charad stresses the shaking response
chathath = be shattered/dismayed; focuses on the shattering effect of fear
arats = to cause dread/be terrifying; emphasizes the threatening source more than the response

word_family.distinction: The verbal root: to be startled by a sudden alarm, to dread. 31 occurrences.
Masculine noun: a testicle (from the sense of trembling/quivering). Only Job 40:17. Same root, different semantic development.

contrast_words.distinction: yirah = reverent fear/awe (the worshipful side); pachad = sudden dread (the visceral side)
batach = to trust/feel secure — the opposite state to pachad's sudden dread
